section.title: One in Christ
section.sectionRationale: Having shown that every believer, whether Jew or Gentile, is saved by grace through faith and not by works, Paul now turns to the great social and cosmic result of that grace. The vertical reconciliation of sinners to God in chapter two produces a horizontal reconciliation of former enemies to one another. The deepest division in the ancient world, the wall between Jew and Gentile, is torn down at the cross, and out of the two God creates one new humanity that is being built into a temple where He Himself dwells.
section.thesis: Those who were once far off, without Christ, without hope, and without God, have been brought near by the blood of Christ, who is Himself our peace: by His death He abolished the dividing wall, made the two into one new man, reconciled both to God in one body, and now builds Jew and Gentile together into a holy temple, a dwelling place for God in the Spirit.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Gentiles remembered in their former estate, once far off and without hope; the peace made by the blood of Christ, who tears down the wall and creates one new man with access to the Father; and the new household of God, built on the apostolic foundation with Christ as cornerstone into a holy temple.

verses.text: Therefore remember that formerly you who are Gentiles in the flesh and called uncircumcised by the so-called circumcision (that done in the body by human hands),
remember that at that time you were separate from Christ, alienated from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers to the covenants of the promise, without hope and without God in the world.

units.label: Brought near, our peace, one new man
units.note: reconciled by the blood of Christ

verses.text: But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far away have been brought near through the blood of Christ.
For He Himself is our {{eirene|peace}}, who has made the two one and has torn down the {{mesotoichon|dividing wall}} of hostility
by abolishing in His flesh the law of commandments and decrees. He did this to create in Himself one new man out of the two, thus making peace
and {{apokatallasso|reconciling}} both of them to God in one body through the cross, by which He put to death their hostility.
He came and preached peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near.
For through Him we both have {{prosagoge|access}} to the Father by one Spirit.
Therefore you are no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of God's household,
built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus Himself as the {{akrogoniaios|cornerstone}}.
In Him the whole building is fitted together and grows into a holy {{naos|temple}} in the Lord.
And in Him you too are being built together into a {{katoiketerion|dwelling place}} for God in His Spirit.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, the restored well-being that comes when hostility is ended and relationship is made right. It carries the fullness of the Hebrew shalom. Paul does not merely say Christ makes peace or gives peace; He Himself is our peace, His own person the meeting point where God and man, Jew and Gentile, are joined. The peace is not a treaty negotiated between two parties but a new reality created in Him.

mesotoichon.gloss: A middle wall, a dividing partition. The image likely recalls the barrier in the Jerusalem temple that separated the court of the Gentiles from the inner courts, past which no Gentile could go on pain of death. It also pictures the whole system of law and custom that fenced Israel off from the nations. Paul says Christ has torn it down, so that the very structure that kept the outsider outside is demolished at the cross.

apokatallasso.gloss: To reconcile fully, to restore a broken relationship to peace. Here the reconciliation is twofold: Jew and Gentile are reconciled to each other and both together are reconciled to God in one body through the cross. The same word crowns the Christ hymn in Colossians 1:20, where God reconciles all things by the blood of the cross. Reconciliation is God's work, achieved at cost, not a peace the estranged parties broker for themselves.

prosagoge.gloss: Access, the right and freedom to approach, the act of being led into the presence of a king. Through Christ both Jew and Gentile have this access to the Father by one Spirit. The word gathers up the whole Trinity into one clause: we come to the Father, through the Son, in the one Spirit. What the temple wall once forbade the Gentile is now opened wide to all who are in Christ.

akrogoniaios.gloss: The cornerstone, or the chief capstone, the decisive foundation stone that sets the line and bears the weight of the whole building, binding its walls together. Christ Jesus Himself is this stone, fulfilling Isaiah 28:16 and Psalm 118:22. The apostles and prophets form the foundation, but Christ is the stone on which everything is aligned and held; remove Him and the structure has no coherence.

naos.gloss: The temple, and specifically the inner sanctuary, the holy dwelling where God's presence rests, not merely the outer temple precincts. Paul says the church, Jew and Gentile joined in Christ, is growing into this holy naos in the Lord. The place of God's dwelling is no longer a building of stone in one city but a people knit together in Christ across every nation.

katoiketerion.gloss: A dwelling place, a settled habitation, a permanent home. The word speaks not of a passing visit but of God taking up lasting residence. The reconciled people are being built together into a katoiketerion for God in His Spirit, the fulfilment of the ancient promise that God would dwell in the midst of His people, now realised by the indwelling Spirit in the church.

basic.context: Paul tells the Gentile believers to remember what they once were: outsiders to Israel, strangers to God's promises, without hope and without God in the world. Then comes the great turn, but now. In Christ Jesus those who were far away have been brought near by His blood. Jesus Himself is their peace. At the cross He tore down the wall that divided Jew and Gentile, made the two into one new humanity, and reconciled both to God in one body. Now they are no longer foreigners but fellow citizens and family, built together into a temple where God Himself lives by His Spirit.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage moves in three beats: the bleak former state of the Gentiles, verses 11 to 12; the peace won at the cross, verses 13 to 18; and the new temple built out of former enemies, verses 19 to 22. The hinge is the phrase but now in verse 13. Paul argues from what God has done in Christ to what the church now is, so the unity of the church is not a goal to achieve by effort but a fact created by the cross to be lived out.
basic.originalLanguages: Christ Himself is our eirene, our peace, the Hebrew shalom embodied in a person. He tore down the mesotoichon, the dividing wall, likely echoing the temple barrier that kept Gentiles out. He is apokatallasso, the one who reconciles both to God in one body. Through Him we have prosagoge, access to the Father by one Spirit. He is the akrogoniaios, the cornerstone that aligns the whole building, which grows into a holy naos, a sanctuary, a katoiketerion, a dwelling place for God in the Spirit.
basic.typology: The temple, its dividing wall, and its holy sanctuary all find their meaning here. The barrier that once shut the Gentile out is torn down in Christ, and the sanctuary where God dwelt is fulfilled in the church, a living temple of Jew and Gentile joined together. The covenants and promises made to Israel are opened to the nations in the one to whom they always pointed.
basic.christ: Christ is not merely the peacemaker; He Himself is our peace. He is the one who brings the far-off near by His blood, who abolishes the dividing wall in His flesh, who creates one new man in Himself, who reconciles both to God through the cross, and who is the cornerstone on whom the whole temple rises. Every clause centres on His person and His death.
basic.god: God is the one who was once far from the nations and now, through the cross, draws near to them. He is the God who reconciles enemies, both to Himself and to one another, and who chooses to dwell not in a building of stone but in a people. The passage shows a God whose purpose was always to gather one people for His own presence.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 1 speaks of the same reconciliation of all things by the blood of the cross. Isaiah 57 promises peace to the far and the near. Galatians 3 declares that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek. 1 Peter 2 calls believers living stones built into a spiritual house.
basic.oneStory: From Abraham, God promised to bless all nations through his seed. Israel was set apart, fenced off by law and temple, to guard the promise until the seed came. Now in Christ the wall comes down and the nations are brought in, one new humanity built into the temple God always meant to fill with His presence. The story runs from a garden where God walked with man to a people in whom God now dwells by His Spirit.

context.literary: This section flows directly out of 2:1 to 10. There Paul described the vertical rescue, dead in sin, made alive by grace through faith. Here he draws out the horizontal result, two peoples made one. The structure turns on remember, verses 11 to 12, but now, verse 13, and therefore, verse 19, moving from past estrangement to present peace to the new corporate identity. The temple imagery of verses 19 to 22 crowns the argument.
context.historical: The division between Jew and Gentile was the deepest fault line of the ancient world. In the Jerusalem temple a low barrier separated the court of the Gentiles from the inner courts, bearing an inscription that threatened death to any Gentile who passed it. Circumcision, food laws, and Sabbath marked Israel off from the nations. Paul, writing to a largely Gentile church in Ephesus, addresses believers who had grown up as outsiders to all of this.
context.canonical: The passage stands with Romans 9 to 11 and Galatians 3 as a central New Testament statement on the place of the Gentiles in God's people. Its temple imagery joins 1 Corinthians 3, 2 Corinthians 6, and 1 Peter 2, where the church is God's dwelling. It fulfils the prophetic hope of Isaiah and the promise to Abraham that in his seed all nations would be blessed.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To make the Gentile believers grasp both the depth of their former exclusion and the greatness of their present inclusion, so that they see the church not as a mixed alliance of two peoples but as one new humanity created at the cross, and live in the unity and peace Christ has already made.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is chiefly a declaration of what God has accomplished: the wall is down, the two are one, both have access, the temple is rising. It is not a program for social improvement but the announcement of a finished work. The abiding application is to remember what we were, to marvel at what Christ has done, and to live as the one reconciled people He has made.

debates.question: What is the law of commandments and decrees that Christ abolished in His flesh?

views.label: The law as the dividing barrier between Jew and Gentile
views.case: In context Paul is describing the wall of hostility, the ceremonial and boundary-marking commands, circumcision, food laws, festivals, that fenced Israel off from the nations. Christ removes what kept the two apart, so the point is not the end of all moral obligation but the removal of the barrier.

views.label: The whole Mosaic law in its condemning and dividing function
views.case: Others read the phrase more broadly: the law as a system, in its power to condemn and to separate, is set aside at the cross, its demands met and its verdict absorbed by Christ, so that it no longer stands between God and man or between Jew and Gentile.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree on Paul's driving point: whatever the law's role in keeping the two apart, Christ has removed it, and out of the two He has made one new man. The passage is not licence for lawlessness but the announcement that the barrier is gone.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the passage is that Christ Himself is our eirene, peace, the embodied shalom in whom hostility ends. He has demolished the mesotoichon, the middle wall of partition, an image that evokes the temple barrier and the whole hedge of law that separated Israel from the nations. The goal of the cross is apokatallasso, to reconcile both to God in one body, killing the hostility. The result is prosagoge, access, the freedom of both peoples to approach the Father by one Spirit. And the church that follows is a building whose akrogoniaios, cornerstone, is Christ Himself, rising into a holy naos, sanctuary, a katoiketerion, a permanent dwelling place for God in the Spirit.

typology.type: The dividing wall of the temple
typology.fulfillment: The barrier that shut the Gentile out of God's presence is torn down in the flesh of Christ, opening access to the Father for all peoples.
The sanctuary where God's presence rested is fulfilled in the church, a living temple of Jew and Gentile joined in Christ and indwelt by the Spirit.
The tested and precious stone promised in Isaiah 28:16 and the rejected stone made head of the corner in Psalm 118:22 are fulfilled in Christ, on whom the whole building is aligned.

study.christ: This passage places the cross at the centre of the church's very existence. Christ Himself is our peace, not simply its broker; the far-off are brought near through His blood; the dividing wall is abolished in His flesh; one new man is created in Himself; both are reconciled to God in one body through the cross; and He is the cornerstone on whom the whole temple rises. There is no unity, no access, no dwelling of God apart from His person and His death. What Israel's temple foreshadowed, Jesus becomes and builds.
study.god: God is the great reconciler, drawing near those who were far off and making enemies into one family. He is a God who takes the initiative at infinite cost, the blood of Christ, to end hostility both vertical and horizontal. And He is the God who dwells, who was never content to be worshipped at a distance but chose to make a people His own home, filling them with His Spirit. The passage reveals a God whose purpose from the beginning was one reconciled people gathered around His presence.

crossReferences.note: The same reconciliation of estranged people to God by the blood of the cross, the sister letter's parallel statement.
Peace, peace, to the far and the near, the prophetic word Paul echoes in verse 17.
In Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, the same abolition of the old division.
Christ the living cornerstone and believers as living stones built into a spiritual house, close kin to verses 20 to 22.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story runs from a God who walked with man in the garden, through a nation set apart and fenced off to guard His promise, to the cross where the fence comes down. Here the ancient promise to Abraham, that in his seed all nations would be blessed, reaches its fulfilment: Jew and Gentile made one new humanity, brought near by blood, built together into the temple God always meant to inhabit. The far-off are home, and God dwells among His people by the Spirit, a foretaste of the city where God is with His people forever.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the Gentiles' former condition in verses 11 to 12?

options.text: Separate from Christ, alienated from Israel, strangers to the covenants, without hope and without God.
options.note: Paul piles up the terms of their exclusion so the reversal will shine all the brighter.

options.text: Slightly disadvantaged but basically near to God already.
options.note: Paul says they were far off, without hope and without God, not merely disadvantaged.

options.text: Members of Israel who had simply forgotten the covenants.
options.note: They were Gentiles in the flesh, outsiders to the commonwealth of Israel, not lapsed members.

options.text: Already reconciled and needing no change.
options.note: The whole point of but now in verse 13 is that a decisive change was needed and came.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he says Christ Himself is our peace?

options.text: Christ in His own person is the peace between God and man and between Jew and Gentile, not merely its negotiator.
options.note: He Himself is our peace, who made the two one; the peace is a reality created in Him.

options.text: Christ arranged a truce between two parties who then keep it themselves.
options.note: The peace is not a brokered treaty but a new reality created in His person at the cross.

options.text: Christ gives inner calm to individuals only.
options.note: The context is corporate: two peoples made one, both reconciled to God.

options.text: Christ leaves the old hostility in place.
options.note: He tore down the dividing wall and put the hostility to death through the cross.

quiz.q: What happened to the dividing wall of hostility at the cross?

options.text: Christ tore it down, abolishing in His flesh what kept Jew and Gentile apart.
options.note: The barrier that fenced the nations out is demolished in the flesh of Christ.

options.text: It was left standing but made a little lower.
options.note: Paul says it was torn down and the hostility put to death, not merely lowered.

options.text: It was rebuilt stronger to keep the peoples separate.
options.note: The movement is the opposite: from two into one new man.

options.text: It never existed in the first place.
options.note: Paul assumes a real division of law and custom that Christ has now removed.

quiz.q: According to verse 18, what do both Jew and Gentile now have through Christ?

options.text: Access to the Father by one Spirit.
options.note: Through Him we both have access to the Father by one Spirit, the whole Trinity in one clause.

options.text: Access to the Father earned by keeping the law.
options.note: Access comes through Christ and by the Spirit, not by law-keeping.

options.text: Access for Jews first and Gentiles only later.
options.note: Paul says we both have access together, by one Spirit.

options.text: No real access, only the hope of it one day.
options.note: The access is a present reality: we both have access to the Father.

quiz.q: What is the church becoming, according to verses 19 to 22?

options.text: A holy temple and dwelling place for God, built on the apostolic foundation with Christ as cornerstone.
options.note: Jew and Gentile fitted together grow into a holy sanctuary where God dwells by His Spirit.

options.text: A loose alliance of two peoples who remain essentially separate.
options.note: Paul says they are one new man, one body, one building, not a loose alliance.

options.text: A physical temple rebuilt in Jerusalem.
options.note: The temple here is a people knit together in Christ, not a building of stone.

options.text: A structure that stands without any need of Christ.
options.note: Christ is the cornerstone on whom the whole building is fitted together and rises.

quiz.q: Why does Paul begin by telling the Gentiles to remember?

options.text: So that grasping how far off they once were, they marvel at how near Christ has brought them.
options.note: Remembering the former estate magnifies the grace of the but now in verse 13.

options.text: So they feel permanently inferior to Jewish believers.
options.note: The aim is not lasting inferiority but shared wonder; both are now one new man with equal access.

options.text: So they return to keeping the ceremonial law.
options.note: Christ abolished the dividing law; the call is to remember grace, not resume the barrier.

options.text: So they take pride in their own achievement.
options.note: The passage removes all boasting; they were brought near by the blood of Christ.

questions: Paul tells the Gentiles to remember what they once were. How does remembering the distance you were once at from God deepen your gratitude for being brought near?
Christ Himself is our peace, not merely the one who arranges it. What difference does it make that peace with God is a person to be received rather than a state you must achieve?
The cross tore down the deepest dividing wall of the ancient world. What walls of hostility, in your church or relationships, is the gospel calling you to treat as already demolished in Christ?
Both Jew and Gentile now have access to the Father by one Spirit. How does this confidence of access shape the way you come to God in prayer?
You are being built together into a dwelling place for God. How does seeing the church as God's living temple change the way you view other believers and your own place among them?

translationNotes.note: The dividing wall renders mesotoichon, a middle wall of partition; many see an allusion to the temple barrier that excluded Gentiles from the inner courts, as well as to the whole hedge of law separating Israel from the nations.

The law of commandments and decrees is best read in context as the boundary-marking legislation that divided Jew from Gentile; whatever its precise scope, Paul's point is that Christ removed it to make one new man out of the two.

Cornerstone renders akrogoniaios, which can mean the foundational corner stone or the chief capstone; either way it is the decisive stone, Christ Himself, that aligns and binds the whole structure, fulfilling Isaiah 28:16 and Psalm 118:22.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 2:11-22 reproduced as supplied. No em or en dashes used; punctuation kept clean per the style rule.
